text.NRSV: They journeyed through an uninhabited wilderness, and pitched their tents in untrodden places.
text.DRB: They made a journey through the deserts, that were not inhabited: and in desert places they pitched cottages.
They stood against the adversaries, and revenged themselves of the enemies.
text.KJV: 11:3 They stood against their enemies, and were auenged of their aduersaries.

text.DRB: They thirsted, and invocated thee, and water was given them out of a most high rock, and quenching of their thirst out of the hard stone.
text.KJV: 11:4 When they were thirsty they called vpon thee, and water was giuen them out of the flinty rocke, and their thirst was quenched out of the hard stone.

text.DRB: For by the things whereby their enemies suffered punishment, for defect of their drink, and therein, when the children of Israel abounded, they did rejoice:
text.KJV: 11:5 For by what things their enemies were punished, by the same they in their neede were benefited.

text.DRB: by these things, when others lacked the same, it went well with them.
text.KJV: 11:6 For in stead of a fountaine of a perpetuall running riuer, troubled with foule blood,

text.DRB: For in stead of the fountain of an everlasting river, thou gavest man's blood to the unjust.
text.KJV: 11:7 For a manifest reproofe of that commandement, whereby the infants were slaine, thou gauest vnto them abundance of water by a meanes which they hoped not for,

text.DRB: Who when they were diminished in the destruction of the murdered infants, thou gavest them aboundant water unlooked for:
text.KJV: 11:8 Declaring by that thirst then, how thou hadst punished their aduersaries.

text.DRB: shewing by the thirst, that then was, how thou didst exalt thine, & didst kill their adversaries.
text.KJV: 11:9 For when they were tryed, albeit but in mercy chastised, they knew how the vngodly were iudged in wrath and tormented thirsting in another maner then the Iust.

text.DRB: For when they were tempted, and indeed with mercy taking discipline, they knew how the impious being judged with wrath did suffer torments.
text.KJV: 11:10 For these thou didst admonish, and trie as a father: but the other as a seuere king thou didst condemne and punish.

text.DRB: These certes admonishing as a father thou didst prove: but them examining as a hard king thou didst condemn.
text.KJV: 11:11 Whether they were absent, or present, they were vexed alike.

text.DRB: For the absent and the present were tormented alike.
text.KJV: 11:12 For a double griefe came vpon them, and a groaning for the remembrance of things past.

text.DRB: For double tediousness had taken them, and sighing with the memory of good things past.
text.KJV: 11:13 For when they heard by their owne punishments the other to be benefited, they had some feeling of the Lord.

text.DRB: For when they understood by their punishment that it went well with them, they remembered our Lord, marveling at the end of the event.
text.KJV: 11:14 For whom they reiected with scorne when hee was long before throwen out at the casting forth of the infants, him in the end, when they saw what came to passe, they admired.

text.DRB: For whom before they derided, being cast forth in that wicked laying out to perish, him they marveled at in the end of the event: not thirsting in like manner to the just.
text.KJV: 11:15 But for the foolish deuises of their wickednesse, wherewith being deceiued, they worshipped serpents voyd of reason, and vile beasts: thou didst send a multitude of vnreasonable beasts vpon them for vengeance,

text.DRB: But for senseless cogitations of their iniquity, for that some erring did worship dumb serpents and superfluous beasts, thou didst send upon them a multitude of dumb beasts for revenge:
text.KJV: 11:16 That they might knowe that wherewithall a man sinneth, by the same also shall he be punished.

text.DRB: that they might know that by what things a man sinneth, by the same also he is tormented.
text.KJV: 11:17 For thy Almighty hand that made the world of matter without forme, wanted not meanes to send among them a multitude of Beares, or fierce Lyons,

text.DRB: For thine omnipotent hand, which made the world of invisible matter, was not unable to send upon them a multitude of bears, or fierce lions,
text.KJV: 11:18 Or vnknowen wild beasts full of rage newly created, breathing out either a fiery vapour, or filthy sents of scattered smoake, or shooting horrible sparkles out of their eyes:

text.DRB: or unknown beasts full of anger of a new kind; or breathing the vapour of fires, or casting forth the savour of smoke, or shooting horrible sparks from their eyes:
text.KJV: 11:19 Whereof not onely the harme might dispatch them at once: but also the terrible sight vtterly destroy them.

text.DRB: of which not only their hurt was able to destroy them, but also their sight to kill them for fear.
text.KJV: 11:20 Yea and without these might they haue fallen downe with one blast, being persecuted of vengeance, and scattered abroad thorough the breath of thy power, but thou hast ordered all things in measure, and number, and weight.
11:21 For thou canst shew thy great strength at all times when thou wilt, and who may withstand the power of thine arme?
text.NABRE: 11:21 For great strength is always present with you; who can resist the might of your arm?
text.NRSV: For it is always in your power to show great strength, and who can withstand the might of your arm?
text.DRB: Yea and without these with one spirit they might have been slain suffering persecution of their own facts, and dispersed by the spirit of thy power: but thou hast disposed all things in measure, and number, and weight.

text.KJV: 11:22 For the whole world before thee is as a litle graine of the ballance, yea as a drop of the morning dew that falleth downe vpon the earth.
text.NABRE: 11:22 Indeed, before you the whole universe is like a grain from a balance, or a drop of morning dew come down upon the earth.
text.NRSV: Because the whole world before you is like a speck that tips the scales, and like a drop of morning dew that falls on the ground.
text.DRB: For, to be of great force rested always in thee only: & who shall resist the power of thine arm?

text.KJV: 11:23 But thou hast mercy vpon all: for thou canst doe all things, and winkest at the sinnes of men: because they should amend.
text.NABRE: 11:23 But you have mercy on all, because you can do all things; and you overlook sins for the sake of repentance.
text.NRSV: But you are merciful to all, for you can do all things, and you overlook people’s sins, so that they may repent.
text.DRB: Because as the least weight of the balance, so is the round world before thee, and as a drop of the dew before day, that falleth upon the earth.

text.KJV: 11:24 For thou louest all the things that are, and abhorrest nothing which thou hast made: for neuer wouldest thou haue made any thing, if thou hadst hated it.
text.NABRE: 11:24 For you love all things that are and loathe nothing that you have made; for you would not fashion what you hate.
text.NRSV: For you love all things that exist, and detest none of the things that you have made, for you would not have made anything if you had hated it.
text.DRB: But thou hast mercy on all, because thou canst do all things, and dissemblest the sins of men for repentance.
For thou lovest all things that are, and hatest nothing of those which thou hast made: for thou didst not ordain or make any thing hating it.
text.KJV: 11:25 And how could any thing haue endured if it had not beene thy will? or beene preserued, if not called by thee?
11:26 But thou sparest all: for they are thine, O Lord, thou louer of soules.
text.NABRE: 11:26 But you spare all things, because they are yours, O Ruler and Lover of souls, for your imperishable spirit is in all things!
text.NRSV: You spare all things, for they are yours, O Lord, you who love the living.
text.DRB: And how could any thing continue, unless thou wouldest? Or be preserved which was not called of thee?
But thou sparest all: because they are thine o Lord, which lovest souls.

text.KJV: 12:1 For thine vncorruptible spirit is in all things.
text.NRSV: For your immortal spirit is in all things.
text.DRB: Ohow good and sweet is thy spirit o Lord in all!

text.KJV: 12:2 Therefore chastnest thou them by little, and little, that offend, and warnest them by putting them in remembrance, wherin they haue offended, that leauing their wickednesse they may beleeue on thee O Lord.
text.NRSV: Therefore you correct little by little those who trespass, and you remind and warn them of the things through which they sin, so that they may be freed from wickedness and put their trust in you, O Lord.
text.DRB: And therefore those that err, by parts thou doest chastise: and doest admonish, and speak to them, concerning the things wherein they sin: that leaving naughtiness, they may believe in thee o Lord.
For those old inhabitants of thy holy land, whom thou didst abhor,
text.KJV: 12:3 For it was thy will to destroy by the handes of our fathers, both those old inhabitants of thy holy land,

text.DRB: because they did works odious to thee by sorceries, and unjust sacrifices,
text.KJV: 12:4 Whom thou hatedst for doing most odious workes of witchcrafts, and wicked sacrifices;

text.DRB: and the murderers of their own children without mercy, and eaters of men's bowels, and devourers of blood from the midst of thy sacrament,
text.KJV: 12:5 And also those mercilesse murderers of children, & deuourers of mans flesh, and the feasts of blood;

text.DRB: and the parents authors of aidless souls, thou wouldst destroy by the hands of our parents,
text.KJV: 12:6 With their Priests out of the midst of their idolatrous crew, and the parents that killed with their owne hands, soules destitute of helpe:

text.DRB: that they might receive a peregrination worthy of the children of God, which is a land of all most dear to thee.
text.KJV: 12:7 That the land which thou esteemedst aboue all other, might receiue a worthy colonie of Gods children.

text.DRB: But them also as men thou didst spare, and didst send forerunners of thine host, wasps, that by little and little they might destroy them.
text.KJV: 12:8 Neuerthelesse, euen those thou sparedst as men, and didst send waspes forerunners of thine hoste, to destroy them by little and little.

text.DRB: Not because thou wast unable in battle to subdue the impious to the just, or with cruel beasts, or with a sharp word to destroy them together:
text.KJV: 12:9 Not that thou wast vnable to bring the vngodly vnder the hand of the righteous in battell, or to destroy them at once with cruel beastes, or with one rough word:

12:10 But executing thy iudgements vpon them by little and little, thou gauest them place of repentance, not being ignorant that they were a naughtie generation, and that their malice, was bred in them, and that their cogitation would neuer be changed.
text.NRSV: But judging them little by little you gave them an opportunity to repent, though you were not unaware that their origin was evil and their wickedness inborn.
text.DRB: but judging by parts thou gavest place of repentance, being not ignorant, that the nation of them is wicked, and their malice natural & that their cogitation could not be changed for ever.
For it was a cursed seed from the beginning: neither fearing any, didst thou give pardon to their sins.
text.KJV: 12:11 For it was a cursed seed, from the beginning, neither didst thou for feare of any man giue them pardon for those things wherein they sinned.

text.DRB: For who shall say to thee: what hast thou done? Or who shall stand against thy judgement? Or who in thy sight shall come revenger of the wicked men? Or who shall impute it to thee, if the nations perish, which thou hast made?
text.KJV: 12:12 For who shall say, What hast thou done? or who shall withstand thy iudgement, or who shall accuse thee for the nations that perish whom thou hast made? or who shall come to stand against thee, to be reuenged for the vnrighteous men?
12:13 For neither is there any God but thou, that careth for all, to whom thou mightest shew that thy iudgement is not vnright.
text.NRSV: For neither is there any god besides you, whose care is for all people, to whom you should prove that you have not judged unjustly.
text.DRB: For there is no other God but thou, who hast care of all, that thou mayst shew that thou doest not give judgement unjustly.
Neither king, nor tyrant in thy sight shall inquire of them, whom thou hast destroyed.
text.KJV: 12:14 Neither shall king or tyrant bee able to set his face against thee, for any whom thou hast punished.
12:15 For so much then as thou art righteous thy selfe, thou orderest all things righteously: thinking it not agreeable with thy power to condemne him þ hath not deserued to be punished.
text.NRSV: But being righteous, you rule all things righteously, deeming it alien to your power to condemn anyone who does not deserve to be punished.
text.DRB: For so much then as thou art just, thou doest dispose all things justly: thou also esteemest it disagreeable from thy power to condemn him, who ought not to be punished.

text.KJV: 12:16 For thy power is the beginning of righteousnesse, and because thou art the Lord of all, it maketh thee to be gracious vnto all.
text.NRSV: For your strength is the source of righteousness, and your sovereignty over all causes you to spare all.
text.DRB: For thy power is the beginning of justice: and for this that thou art Lord of all, thou makest thyself to spare all.
For thou shewest power, which art not thought to be absolute in power, and thou convincest the boldness of them, that know thee not.
text.KJV: 12:17 For when men will not beleeue, that thou art of a full power, thou shewest thy strength, and among them that know it, thou makest their boldnesse manifest.

text.DRB: But thou dominatour of power, judgest with tranquility, and with great reverence disposest of us: for it is in thy power when thou wilt, to be able.
text.KJV: 12:18 But thou, mastering thy power, iudgest with equitie, and orderest vs with great fauour: for thou mayest vse power when thou wilt.

text.DRB: And thou hast taught thy people by such works, that they must be just and gentle, and hast made thy children of good hope: because judging thou givest in sins place of repentance.
text.KJV: 12:19 But by such workes hast thou taught thy people, that the iust man should be mercifull, and hast made thy children to be of a good hope, that thou giuest repentance for sinnes.

text.DRB: For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy servants, and that deserved to die, with so great consideration, giving time and place, whereby they might be changed from their wickedness:
text.KJV: 12:20 For if thou didst punish the enemies of thy children, and the condemned to death with such deliberation, giuing them time and place, wherby they might be deliuered from their malice.

text.DRB: with what diligence hast thou judged thy children, to whose parents thou gavest oaths and covenants of good promises?
text.KJV: 12:21 With how great circumspection diddest thou iudge thine owne sonnes, vnto whose fathers thou hast sworne, and made couenants of good promises?

text.DRB: Therefore when thou givest us discipline, thou scourgest our enemies very many ways, that judging we may think upon thy goodness: and when we are judged, we may hope for thy mercy.
text.KJV: 12:22 Therefore whereas thou doest chasten vs, thou scourgest our enemies a thousand times more, to the intent that when wee iudge, wee should carefully thinke of thy goodnesse, and when we our selues are iudged, wee should looke for mercy.

text.DRB: Wherefore to them also, which in their life have lived foolishly & unjustly, thou hast given great torments by the same things, which they did worship.
text.KJV: 12:23 Wherefore, whereas men haue liued dissolutely and vnrighteously, thou hast tormented them with their owne abominations.

text.DRB: For they wandered long in the way of error, esteeming for gods those things, that in beasts are superfluous, living after the manner of senseless infants.
text.KJV: 12:24 For they went astray very farre in the wayes of errour, & held them for gods (which euen amongst the beasts of their enemies were despised) being deceiued as children of no vnderstanding.

text.DRB: For this cause thou hast given judgement on them as on senseless children to be in derision.
text.KJV: 12:25 Therefore vnto them, as to children without the vse of reason, thou didst send a iudgement to mocke them.

text.DRB: But they that were not amended by scorns and reprehensions, have tried the worthy judgment of God.
text.KJV: 12:26 But they that would not bee refourmed by that correction wherein he dallied with them, shall feele a iudgement worthy of God.

text.DRB: For in what things they suffering took indignation, by those whom they thought gods, when seeing they were destroyed in them, him, whom in time past they denied that they knew, they acknowledged the true God: for the which cause the end also of their condemnation shall come upon them.
text.KJV: 12:27 For looke, for what things they grudged when they were punished, (that is) for them whom they thought to be gods,  being punished in them; when they saw it, they acknowledged him to be the true God, whome before they denyed to know: and therefore came extreme damnation vpon them.

13:1 Surely vaine are all men by nature, who are ignorant of God, and could not out of the good things that are seene, know him that is: neither by considering the workes, did they acknowledge the worke-master;
text.NABRE: 13:1 Chapter 13 Digression on False Worship A. Nature Worship Foolish by nature were all who were in ignorance of God, and who from the good things seen did not succeed in knowing the one who is, and from studying the works did not discern the artisan;
text.NRSV: For all people who were ignorant of God were foolish by nature; and they were unable from the good things that are seen to know the one who exists, nor did they recognize the artisan while paying heed to his works;
text.DRB: But all men be vain, in whom there is not the knowledge of God: and of these good things which are seen they could not understand him that is, neither attending to the works have they agnised who was the workman:

text.KJV: 13:2 But deemed either fire, or wind, or the swift aire, or the circle of the stars, or the violent water, or the lights of heauen to be the gods which gouerne the world:
text.NABRE: 13:2 Instead either fire, or wind, or the swift air, or the circuit of the stars, or the mighty water, or the luminaries of heaven, the governors of the world, they considered gods.
text.NRSV: but they supposed that either fire or wind or swift air, or the circle of the stars, or turbulent water, or the luminaries of heaven were the gods that rule the world.
text.DRB: but either the fire, or the wind, or the swift air, or a circle of stars, or exceeding much water, or the sun and moon, they thought to be gods, rulers of the world.

text.KJV: 13:3 With whose beautie, if they being delighted, tooke them to be gods: let them know how much better the Lord of them is; for the first Author of beautie hath created them.
text.NABRE: 13:3 Now if out of joy in their beauty they thought them gods, let them know how far more excellent is the Lord than these; for the original source of beauty fashioned them.
text.NRSV: If through delight in the beauty of these things people assumed them to be gods, let them know how much better than these is their Lord, for the author of beauty created them.
text.DRB: With whose beauty if being delighted they thought them gods: let them know how much the Lord of them is more beautiful than they. For the author of beauty made all those things.
Or if they marveled at their virtue, & operations, let them understand by them, that he which made these, is stronger than they:
text.KJV: 13:4 But if they were astonished at their power and vertue, let them vnderstand by them, how much mightier he is that made them.
13:5 For by the greatnesse and beautie of the creatures, proportionably the Maker of them is seene.
text.NABRE: 13:5 For from the greatness and the beauty of created things their original author, by analogy, is seen.
text.NRSV: For from the greatness and beauty of created things comes a corresponding perception of their Creator.
text.DRB: for by the greatness of the beauty, and of the creature, the Creator of them may be seen, to be known thereby.
But notwithstanding there is yet in these less complaint. For they also perhaps err, seeking God, and desirous to find him.
text.KJV: 13:6 But yet for this they are the lesse to bee blamed: for they peraduenture erre seeking God, and desirous to finde him.

text.DRB: For whereas they converse in his works, they inquire: & they are persuaded that the things be good which are seen.
text.KJV: 13:7 For being conuersant in his workes, they search him diligently, and beleeue their sight: because the things are beautifull that are seene.

text.DRB: But again neither ought these to be pardoned.
text.KJV: 13:8 Howbeit, neither are they to bee pardoned.

text.DRB: For if they could know so much, that they were able to estimate the world: how did they not more easily find the Lord thereof?
text.KJV: 13:9 For if they were able to know so much, that they could aime at the world; how did they not sooner finde out the Lord thereof?

13:10 But miserable are they, and in dead things is their hope, who called them gods which are the workes of mens hands, golde and siluer, to shewe arte in, and resemblances of beasts, or a stone good for nothing, the worke of an ancient hand.
text.NABRE: 13:10 B. Idolatry But wretched are they, and in dead things are their hopes, who termed gods things made by human hands: Gold and silver, the product of art, and images of beasts, or useless stone, the work of an ancient hand.
text.NRSV: But miserable, with their hopes set on dead things, are those who give the name “gods” to the works of human hands, gold and silver fashioned with skill.
text.DRB: But they are unhappy, & their hope is among the dead, who have called the works of men's hands gods, gold & silver, the invention of art, and the similitudes of beasts, or an unprofitable stone the work of an old hand.

Or if an artificer a carpenter, cut straight timber out of the wood, & pare off all the bark thereof cunningly, and using his art diligently frameth a vessel profitable for the common use of this life,
text.KJV: 13:11 Now a carpenter that felleth timber, after hee hath sawen downe a tree meet for the purpose, and taken off all the barke skilfully round about, and hath wrought it handsomely, & made a vessell thereof fit for the seruice of mans life:

text.DRB: and useth the chips of that work to dress his meat:
text.KJV: 13:12 And after spending the refuse of his worke to dresse his meat, hath filled himselfe:

text.DRB: and maketh that which is left thereof, which is for no uses, but being a crooked piece of wood, and full of knobs, carveth it diligently in the hollowness thereof, and by the skill of his art fashioneth it, and maketh it like to the image of a man,
text.KJV: 13:13 And taking the very refuse among those which serued to no vse (being a crooked piece of wood, and ful of knots) hath carued it diligently when hee had nothing else to doe, and formed it by the skill of his vnderstanding, and fashioned it to the image of a man:

text.DRB: or compareth it to some beast, straking it over with red, and with painting making the colour thereof ruddy, and layeth a colour over every spot that is in it:
text.KJV: 13:14 Or made it like some vile beast, laying it ouer with vermilion, and with paint, colouring it red, and couering euery spot therein:

text.DRB: and maketh a worthy habitation for it, and setting it in a wall, and fastening it with iron
text.KJV: 13:15 And when he had made a conuenient roume for it, set it in a wall, and made it fast with yron:

text.DRB: lest perhaps it fall, providing for it, knowing that it cannot help itself: for it is an image, and it needeth help.
text.KJV: 13:16 For he prouided for it, that it might not fall: knowing that it was vnable to helpe it selfe, (for it is an image and hath neede of helpe:)

text.DRB: And concerning his substance, & his children, & for marriage, making a vow he seeketh to it. He is not ashamed to speak with him, that is without soul:
text.KJV: 13:17 Then maketh hee prayer for his goods, for his wife and children, and is not ashamed to speake to that which hath no life.

text.DRB: and for health certes he beseecheth the weak, and for life asketh the dead, and for help invocateth him that is unprofitable:
text.KJV: 13:18 For health, hee calleth vpon that which is weake: for life, prayeth to that which is dead: for aide, humbly beseecheth that which hath least meanes to helpe: and for a good iourney, hee asketh of that which cannot set a foot forward:

text.DRB: and for a journey asketh him, that cannot walk: and for getting, and for working, and for the event of all things he asketh him, that in all is unprofitable.
text.KJV: 13:19 And for gaining and getting, and for good successe of his hands, asketh abilitie to doe, of him that is most vnable to doe any thing.

text.DRB: Again another thinking to sail, and beginning to make a journey through the fierce waves, invocateth wood more frail than the wood that carrieth him.
text.KJV: 14:1 Againe, one preparing himselfe to saile, and about to passe through the raging waues, calleth vpon a piece of wood more rotten then the vesell that carieth him.

text.DRB: For covetousness of getting invented it, and the craftsman by his wisdom framed it.
text.KJV: 14:2 For verely desire of gaine deuised that, and the workeman built it by his skill:

text.DRB: But thy providence, o Father, doth govern: because thou hast given a way even in the sea, and among the waves a most sure path,
text.KJV: 14:3 But thy prouidence, O Father, gouerneth it: for thou hast made a way in the Sea, and a safe path in the waues:

text.DRB: shewing that thou art able to save out of all things, yea if a man go to the sea without art.
text.KJV: 14:4 Shewing that thou canst saue from all danger: yea though a man went to Sea without arte.

text.DRB: But that thy works might not be void of wisdom: for this cause also men commit their lives even to a little wood, and passing over the sea are delivered by ship.
text.KJV: 14:5 Neuerthelesse thou wouldest not that the works of thy wisedome should be idle, and therefore doe men commit their liues to a small piece of wood, and passing the rough sea in a weake vessell, are saued.

text.DRB: But from the beginning also when the proud giants perished, the hope of the world flying to a ship, rendered to the world seed of nativity, which was governed by thy hand.
text.KJV: 14:6 For in the old time also when the proud gyants perished, the hope of the world gouerned by thy hand, escaped in a weake vessell, and left to all ages a seed of generation.

text.DRB: For blessed is the wood, by the which justice is made.
text.KJV: 14:7 For blessed is the wood, whereby righteousnesse commeth.

text.DRB: But the idol that is made by hands, cursed is both it, and he that made it: because he indeed wrought it: and the same being frail, was called God.
text.KJV: 14:8 But that which is made with hands, is cursed, aswell it, as hee that made it: he, because he made it, and it, because being corruptible it was called God.

text.DRB: But to God the impious and his impiety are odious alike.
text.KJV: 14:9 For the vngodly and his vngodlines are both alike hatefull vnto God.

text.DRB: For that which is made, with him that made it, shall suffer torments.
text.KJV: 14:10 For that which is made, shall bee punished together with him that made it.

text.DRB: For this cause also in the idol of the nations there shall be no respect: because the creatures of God were made to hatred, and for tentation to the souls of men, and for a snare to the feet of the unwise.
text.KJV: 14:11 Therfore euen vpon the idoles of the Gentiles shall there be a visitation: because in the creature of God they are become an abomination and stumbling blocks to the soules of men, and a snare to the feet of the vnwise.
14:12 For the deuising of idoles was the beginning of spiritual fornication, and the inuention of them the corruption of life.
text.NABRE: 14:12 The Origin and Evils of Idolatry For the source of wantonness is the devising of idols; and their invention, a corruption of life.
text.NRSV: For the idea of making idols was the beginning of fornication, and the invention of them was the corruption of life.
text.DRB: For the beginning of fornication is the devising of idols: and the inventing of them is the corruption of life.

text.KJV: 14:13 For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for euer.
text.NABRE: 14:13 For in the beginning they were not, nor can they ever continue;
text.NRSV: for they did not exist from the beginning, nor will they last forever.
text.DRB: For neither were they from the beginning, neither shall they be for ever.
For this vanity of men came into the world: and therefore there is found a short end of them.
text.KJV: 14:14 For by the vaine glory of men they entred into the world, and therefore shall they come shortly to an end.

text.DRB: For the father being sorrowful with bitter mourning, made unto himself the image of his son quickly taken away: and him, that then was a dead man, now he began to worship as God, and appointed holy things and sacrifices among his servants.
text.KJV: 14:15 For a father afflicted with vntimely mourning, when he hath made an image of his childe soone taken away, now honoured him as a god, which was then a dead man, and deliuered to those that were vnder him, ceremonies and sacrifices.

text.DRB: Afterward by succession of time, the wicked custom prevailing, this error was kept as a law, and things graven were worshipped by the commandment of Tyrants.
text.KJV: 14:16 Thus in processe of time an vngodly custome growen strong, was kept as a law, and grauen images were worshipped by the commandements of kings,

text.DRB: And those, whom openly men could not honour, for that they were far off, their figure being brought from a far, they made an evident image of the king, whom they would honour: that by their carefulness they might honour as present, him that was absent.
text.KJV: 14:17 Whom men could not honour in presence, because they dwelt farre off, they tooke the counterfeit of his visage from farre, and made an expresse image of a king whom they honoured, to the end that by this their forwardnes, they might flatter him that was absent, as if he were present.

text.DRB: And to the worshipping of these, the excellent diligence also of the artificer, holp them forward, that were ignorant.
text.KJV: 14:18 Also the singular diligence of the artificer did helpe to set forward the ignorant to more superstition.

text.DRB: For he willing to please him that entertained him, laboured by his art to fashion the similitude in better sort.
text.KJV: 14:19 For he peraduenture willing to please one in authoritie, forced all his skill to make the resemblance of the best fashion.

text.DRB: And the multitude of men carried away by the beauty of the work, him that a little before had been honoured as a man, now they esteemed for a God.
text.KJV: 14:20 And so the multitude allured by the grace of the worke, tooke him now for a god, which a litle before was but honoured as a man.

text.DRB: And this was the deceiving of man's life: because men serving either affection, or kings, gave the name that is not communicable, to stones and wood.
text.KJV: 14:21 And this was an occasion to deceiue the world: for men seruing either calamitie or tyrannie, did ascribe vnto stones, and stockes, the incommunicable Name.

text.DRB: And it was not sufficient that they erred about the knowledge of God, but also living in a great battle of ignorance, so many and so great evils they call peace.
text.KJV: 14:22 Moreouer this was not enough for them, that they erred in the knowledge of God, but whereas they liued in the great warre of ignorance, those so great plagues called they peace.

text.DRB: For either sacrificing their children, or making obscure sacrifices, or having watches full of madness,
text.KJV: 14:23 For whilest they slew their children in sacrifices, or vsed secret ceremonies, or made reuellings of strange rites

text.DRB: they now neither keep life, nor marriage clean, but one killeth another by envy, or playing the adulterer maketh him sorrowful:
text.KJV: 14:24 They kept neither liues nor mariages any longer vndefiled: but either one slew another traiterously, or grieued him by adulterie:

text.DRB: and all things are mingled together, blood, manslaughter, theft and fiction, corruption and infidelity, trouble and perjury, disquieting of the good,
text.KJV: 14:25 So that there reigned in all men without exception, blood, manslaughter, theft, and dissimulation, corruption, vnfaithfulnesse, tumults, periurie,

text.DRB: forgetfulness of God, inquination of souls, immutation of nativity, inconstancy of marriage, disorder of adultery, and unchasteness.
text.KJV: 14:26 Disquieting of good men, forgetfulnesse of good turnes, defiling of soules, changing of kinde, disorder in mariages, adulterie, and shameles vncleannesse.
14:27 For the worshipping of idoles not to be named, is the beginning, the cause, and the end of all euill.
text.NABRE: 14:27 For the worship of infamous idols is the reason and source and extreme of all evil.
text.NRSV: For the worship of idols not to be named is the beginning and cause and end of every evil.
text.DRB: For the worship of idols not to be named, is the cause of all evil, and the beginning and end.
For either when they rejoice, they are mad: or certes prophesy false things, or live unjustly, or quickly forswear themselves.
text.KJV: 14:28 For either they are mad when they be merry, or prophesie lies, or liue vniustly, or else lightly forsweare themselues.

text.DRB: For whilst they trust in idols, which are without soul, swearing amiss they hope not to be hurt.
text.KJV: 14:29 For insomuch as their trust is in idoles which haue no life, though they sweare falsly, yet they looke not to bee hurt.

text.DRB: Two evil things therefore shall happen to them worthily, because they have thought evil of God, attending to idols, and have sworn unjustly, in guile contemning justice.
text.KJV: 14:30 Howbeit for both causes shal they be iustly punished: both because they thought not well of God, giuing heed vnto idols, and also vniustly swore in deceit, despising holinesse.

text.DRB: For it is not the power of them that are sworn by, but the punishment of them that sin goeth always through the transgression of the unjust.
text.KJV: 14:31 For it is not the power of them by whom they sweare: but it is the iust vengeance of sinners, that punisheth alwayes the offence of the vngodly.
15:1 But thou O God, art gracious and true: long suffering, and in mercy ordering all things.
text.NABRE: 15:1 Chapter 15 But you, our God, are good and true, slow to anger, and governing all with mercy.
text.NRSV: But you, our God, are kind and true, patient, and ruling all things in mercy.
text.DRB: But o thou our God art sweet and true, patient, and disposing all things in mercy.

text.KJV: 15:2 For if we sinne we are thine, knowing thy power: but we will not sinne, knowing that we are counted thine.
text.NABRE: 15:2 For even if we sin, we are yours, and know your might; but we will not sin, knowing that we belong to you.
text.NRSV: For even if we sin we are yours, knowing your power; but we will not sin, because we know that you acknowledge us as yours.
text.DRB: For if we sin, we are thine, knowing thy greatness: and if we sin not, we know that we are counted with thee.

text.KJV: 15:3 For to know thee is perfect righteousnesse: yea to know thy power is the roote of immortality.
text.NABRE: 15:3 For to know you well is complete righteousness, and to know your might is the root of immortality.
text.NRSV: For to know you is complete righteousness, and to know your power is the root of immortality.
text.DRB: For to know thee, is absolute justice: and to know justice, and thy power, is the root of immortality.
For men's invention of evil art hath not brought us into error, nor the shadow of a picture being a labour without fruit, a shape graven by diverse colours,
text.KJV: 15:4 For neither did the mischieuous inuention of men deceiue vs: nor an image spotted with diuers colours, the painters fruitlesse labour.

text.DRB: the sight whereof giveth concupiscence to the senseless, and he loveth the shape without life of a dead image.
text.KJV: 15:5 The sight wherof entiseth fooles to lust after it, and so they desire the forme of a dead image that hath no breath.

text.DRB: The lovers of evils are worthy to have their hope in such things, both they that make them, and that love, and that worship them.
text.KJV: 15:6 Both they that make them, they that desire them, and they that worship them, are louers of euill things, and are worthy to haue such things to trust vpon.

text.DRB: Yea and the potter pressing soft earth, with labour fashioneth every vessel to our uses, and of the same clay maketh the vessels that are clean to use, and in like manner them, that are contrary to these: but what the use of these vessels is, the potter is judge.
text.KJV: 15:7 For the potter tempering soft earth fashioneth, euery vessell with much labour for our seruice: yea of the same clay hee maketh both the vessels that serue for cleane vses: and likewise also all such as serue to the contrary: but what is the vse of either sort, the potter himselfe is the iudge.

text.DRB: And with vain labour he fashioneth a god of the same clay: he which a little before was made of earth, and a little after returneth back whence he was taken, being exacted the debt of the life which he had.
text.KJV: 15:8 And employing his labours lewdly, he maketh a vaine God of the same clay, euen he which a little before was made of earth himselfe, and within a little while after returneth to the same out of the which he was taken: when his life which was lent him shall be demanded.

text.DRB: But his care is, not because he shall labour, nor because he hath a short life, but he contendeth with gold-smiths and silver-smiths: yea and he imitateth the copper-smiths, and counteth it a glory, because he maketh vain things.
text.KJV: 15:9 Notwithstanding his care is, not that hee shall haue much labour, nor that his life is short: but striueth to excel goldsmiths, and siluersmiths, and endeuoureth to doe like the workers in brasse, and counteth it his glory to make counterfeit things.

text.DRB: For his heart is ashes, and his hope vain earth, and his life viler than clay:
text.KJV: 15:10 His heart is ashes, his hope is more vile then earth, and his life of lesse value then clay:

text.DRB: because he was ignorant who made him, and who inspired into him the soul which worketh, & who breathed into him the vital spirit.
text.KJV: 15:11 Forasmuch as hee knew not his maker, and him that inspired into him an actiue soule, and breathed in a liuing spirit.

text.DRB: Yea and they esteemed our life to be a pastime, and the conversation of life made for a gain, & that we must get every way even of evil.
text.KJV: 15:12 But they counted our life a pastime, & our time here a market for gaine: for, say they, we must be getting euery way, though it be by euil meanes.

text.DRB: For he knoweth that he offendeth above all men, which of the matter of earth fashioneth frail vessels, and sculptiles.
text.KJV: 15:13 For this man that of earthly matter maketh brickle vessels, and grauen images, knoweth himselfe to offend aboue all others.

text.DRB: For all the unwise and unhappy above measure of the soul, proud are the enemies of thy people, and rule over them:
text.KJV: 15:14 And all the enemies of thy people, that hold them in subiection are most foolish and are more miserable then very babes.

text.DRB: because they have esteemed all the idols of the nations for gods, which neither have use of eyes to see, nor nostrils to take breath, nor ears to hear, nor fingers of the hands to handle, yea and their feet are slow to walk.
text.KJV: 15:15 For they counted all the idoles of the heathen to be gods: which neither haue the vse of eyes to see, nor noses to draw breath, nor eares to heare, nor fingers of hands to handle, and as for their feete they are slow to goe.

text.DRB: For a man made them: and he that borrowed breath, the same fashioned them. For no man can make God like to himself.
text.KJV: 15:16 For man made them, and he that borrowed his owne spirit fashioned them, but no man can make a god like vnto himselfe.

text.DRB: For whereas himself is mortal, he maketh a dead thing with his wicked hands. For he is better than they, whom he worshippeth, because he indeed lived, though he were mortal, but they never.
text.KJV: 15:17 For being mortall he worketh a dead thing with wicked hands: for hee himselfe is better then the things which he worshippeth: whereas he liued once, but they neuer.

text.DRB: But they worship also most miserable beasts: for the senseless things compared to these, are worse than they.
text.KJV: 15:18 Yea they worshipped those beasts also that are most hatefull: for being compared together, some are worse then others.

text.DRB: Yea neither by sight can any man see good of these beasts, But they have fled from the praise of God, and from his blessing.
text.KJV: 15:19 Neither are they beautifull, so much, as to bee desired in respect of beasts, but they went without the praise of God and his blessing.

text.DRB: For these things, and by the like to these, they have worthily suffered torments, and were destroyed by a multitude of beasts.
text.KJV: 16:1 Therefore by the like were they punished worthily, and by the multitude of beasts tormented.

text.DRB: For the which torments thou didst well dispose of thy people, to whom thou gavest the desire of their delectation, a new taste, preparing them the quail for meat:
text.KJV: 16:2 In stead of which punishment, dealing graciously with thine owne people thou preparedst for them meate of a strange taste: euen quailes to stirre vp their appetite:

text.DRB: that they indeed coveting meat, because of those things which were shewed and sent them, might be turned away even from necessary concupiscence. But they in short time being made needy, tasted a new meat.
text.KJV: 16:3 To the end that they desiring food might for the ougly sight of the beasts sent among them, loath euen that which they must needs desire: but these suffering penury for a short space, might be made partakers of a strange taste.

text.DRB: For it behoved that without excuse destruction should come upon them exercising tyranny: but to these only to shew how their enemies were destroyed.
text.KJV: 16:4 For it was requisite, that vpon them excercising tyranny should come penury which they could not auoyde: but to these it should onely be shewed how their enemies were tormented.

text.DRB: For when the cruel wrath of beasts came upon them, they were destroyed with the bitings of perverse serpents.
text.KJV: 16:5 For when the horrible fiercenesse of beasts came vpon these, and they perished with the stings of crooked serpents, thy wrath endured not for euer.

text.DRB: Howbeit thy wrath endured not for ever, but for chastisement they were troubled a short time, having a sign of salvation for the remembrance of the commandment of thy law.
text.KJV: 16:6 But they were troubled for a smal season that they might be admonished, hauing a signe of saluation, to put them in remembrance of the commandement of thy Law.
16:7 For hee that turned himselfe towards it, was not saued by the thing that he saw: but by thee that art the sauiour of all.
text.NABRE: 16:7 For the one who turned toward it was saved, not by what was seen, but by you, the savior of all.
text.NRSV: For the one who turned toward it was saved, not by the thing that was beheld, but by you, the Savior of all.
text.DRB: For he that turned to it, was not healed by that which he saw, but by thee the Saviour of all:
and in this thou didst shew to our enemies, that thou art he which deliverest from all evil.
text.KJV: 16:8 And in this thou madest thine enemies confesse, that it is thou who deliuerest from all euill:

text.DRB: For the bitings of locusts and flies killed them, and their was found no remedy for their life: because they were worthy to be destroyed by such things.
text.KJV: 16:9 For them the bitings of grassehoppers and flies killed, neither was there found any remedy for their life: for they were worthy to bee punished by such.

text.DRB: But neither the teeth of venomous dragons overcame thy children: for thy mercy coming healed them.
text.KJV: 16:10 But thy sonnes, not the very teeth of venemous dragons ouercame: for thy mercy was euer by them, and healed them.

text.DRB: For in memory of thy words they were examined, & were quickly saved, lest falling into deep oblivion they might not use thy help.
text.KJV: 16:11 For they were pricked, that they should remember thy words, and were quickly saued, that not falling into deep forgetfulnesse, they might be continually mindefull of thy goodnesse.
16:12 For it was neither herbe, nor mollifying plaister that restored them to health: but thy word, O Lord, which healeth all things.
text.NABRE: 16:12 For indeed, neither herb nor application cured them, but your all-healing word, O Lord!
text.NRSV: For neither herb nor poultice cured them, but it was your word, O Lord, that heals all people.
text.DRB: For neither herb, nor plaster healed them, but thy word, o Lord, which healeth all things.

text.KJV: 16:13 For thou hast power of life and death: thou leadest to the gates of hell, and bringest vp againe.
text.NABRE: 16:13 For you have dominion over life and death; you lead down to the gates of Hades and lead back.
text.NRSV: For you have power over life and death; you lead mortals down to the gates of Hades and back again.
text.DRB: For it is thou, o Lord, that hast power of life and death, and bringest down to the gates of death, and fetchest again:
but man certes killeth by malice, and when the spirit is gone forth, it shall not return, neither shall he call back the soul that is received:
text.KJV: 16:14 A man indeed killeth through his malice: and the spirit when it is gone foorth returneth not; neither the soule receiued vp, commeth againe.

text.DRB: but it is unpossible to escape thy hand.
text.KJV: 16:15 But it is not possible to escape thine hand.

text.DRB: For the impious denying to know thee, have been scourged by the strength of thine arm, suffering persecution by strange waters, and hail, and rain, and consumed by fire.
text.KJV: 16:16 For the vngodly that denied to know thee, were scourged by the strength of thine arme: with strange raines, hailes, and showers were they persecuted, that they could not auoyd, and through fire were they consumed.

text.DRB: For that which was marvelous in water, which extinguisheth all things, fire more prevailed: for the world is revenger of the just.
text.KJV: 16:17 For, which is most to be wondered at, the fire had more force in the water that quencheth all things: for the world fighteth for the righteous.

text.DRB: For a certain time, the fire was mitigated, that the beasts which were sent to the impious might not be burnt, but that they seeing might know that by God's judgment they suffer punishment.
text.KJV: 16:18 For sometimes the flame was mitigated, that it might not burne vp the beasts that were sent against the vngodly: but themselues might see and perceiue that they were persecuted with the iudgement of God.

text.DRB: And at a certain time the fire above his power burnt in water on every side, that it might destroy the nation of a wicked land.
text.KJV: 16:19 And at another time it burneth euen in the midst of water, aboue the power of fire, that it might destroy the fruits of an vniust land.

16:20 In stead whereof thou feddest thine owne people, with Angels food, and didst send them from heauen bread prepared without their labour, able to content euery mans delight, and agreeing to euery taste.
text.NABRE: 16:20 Instead of this, you nourished your people with food of angels and furnished them bread from heaven, ready to hand, untoiled-for, endowed with all delights and conforming to every taste.
text.NRSV: Instead of these things you gave your people food of angels, and without their toil you supplied them from heaven with bread ready to eat, providing every pleasure and suited to every taste.
text.DRB: For the which things thou didst nourish thy people with the meat of Angels, and bread prepared thou gavest them from heaven without labour, having in it all delectation, and the sweetness of all taste.
For thy substance did shew thy sweetness which thou hast toward thy children, and serving every man's will, it was turned to that, that every man would.
text.KJV: 16:21 For thy sustenance declared thy sweetnesse vnto thy children, and seruing to the appetite of the eater tempered it selfe to euery mans liking.

text.DRB: Yea snow and ice sustained the force of fire, and melted not: that they might know that fire burning in hail, and lightening in rain destroyed the fruits of the enemies.
text.KJV: 16:22 But snow and yce endured the fire and melted not, that they might know that fire burning the haile, and sparkling in the raine, did destroy the fruits of the enemies.

text.DRB: And this again, that the just might be nourished, it forgat also his own strength.
text.KJV: 16:23 But this againe did euen forget his owne strength, that the righteous might be nourished.

text.DRB: For the creature serving thee the Creator, is fierce into torment against the unjust: and is made more gentle to do good for them, that trust in thee.
text.KJV: 16:24 For the creature that serueth thee who art the maker, encreaseth his strength against the vnrighteous for their punishment, and abateth his strength for the benefit of such as put their trust in thee.

text.DRB: For this cause even then being transformed into all things, they served thy grace the nurse of all, at their will that desired thee:
text.KJV: 16:25 Therefore euen then was it altered into all fashions, and was obedient to thy grace that nourisheth all things, according to the desire of them that had need:

text.DRB: that thy children might know, whom thou lovedst o Lord, that not the fruits of nativity do feed men, but thy word preserveth them, that believe in thee.
text.KJV: 16:26 That thy children, O Lord, whom thou louest, might know that it is not the growing of fruits that nourisheth man: but that it is thy word which preserueth them that put their trust in thee.

text.DRB: For that which could not be destroyed by fire, forthwith being heated with a little beam of the sun did melt:
text.KJV: 16:27 For that which was not destroied of the fire, being warmed with a litle Sunne beame, soone melted away,

text.DRB: that it might be known to all men, that we ought to prevent the sun to bless thee, and at the rising of light to adore thee.
text.KJV: 16:28 That it might bee knowen, that wee must preuent the Sunne, to giue thee thanks, and at the day-spring pray vnto thee.

text.DRB: For the faith of the ungrateful shall melt as winter ice, & shall perish as unprofitable water.
text.KJV: 16:29 For the hope of the vnfaithfull, shal melt away as the Winters hoarefrost, and shall runne away as vnprofitable water.
17:1 For great are thy Iudgements, and cannot be expressed: therefore vnnourtured soules haue erred.
text.NABRE: 17:1 Chapter 17 Fourth Example: Darkness Afflicts the Egyptians, While the Israelites Have Light For great are your judgments, and hard to describe; therefore the unruly souls went astray.
text.NRSV: Great are your judgments and hard to describe; therefore uninstructed souls have gone astray.
text.DRB: For thy judgements, o Lord, are great, & thy words inexplicable, for this cause the souls lacking discipline have erred.

For whilst the wicked are persuaded that they can rule over the holy nation: fettered with the bands of darkness and long night, shut up under roofs, they have lien fugitives from the everlasting providence.
text.KJV: 17:2 For when vnrighteous men thought to oppresse the holy nation: they being shut vp in their houses, the prisoners of darkenesse, and fettered with the bondes of a long night, lay  exiled from the eternall prouidence.

text.DRB: And whilst they think that they lie hid in obscure sins, they were dispersed by the dark covert of oblivion, being horribly afraid and disturbed with exceeding admiration.
text.KJV: 17:3 For while they supposed to lie hid in their secret sinnes, they were scattered vnder a darke vaile of forgetfulnesse, being horribly astonished, and troubled with (strange) apparitions.

text.DRB: For neither did the den that contained them, keep them without fear: because the sound coming down troubled them, and sorrowful visions appearing to them, put them in fear.
text.KJV: 17:4 For neither might the corner that helde them keepe them from feare: but noises (as of waters) falling downe, sounded about them, and sadde visions appeared vnto them with heauie countenances.

text.DRB: And no force certes of the fire could give them light, neither could the clear flames of the stars lighten that horrible night.
text.KJV: 17:5 No power of the fire might giue them light: neither could the bright flames of the starres endure to lighten that horrible night.

text.DRB: But there appeared to them sudden fire full of fear: and being strucken with the fear of that face, which was not seen, they esteemed the things that were seen to be worse.
text.KJV: 17:6 Onely there appeared vnto them a fire kindled of it selfe, very dreadfull: for being much terrified, they thought the things which they saw to be worse then the sight they saw not.

text.DRB: and there were added derisions of the magical art, and contumelious rebuke of the glory of their wisdom.
text.KJV: 17:7 As for the illusions of arte Magicke, they were put downe, and their vaunting in wisedome was reprooued with disgrace.

text.DRB: For they which promised that they would expel fears and perturbations from the languishing soul, these with derision languished full of fear.
text.KJV: 17:8 For they that promised to driue away terrours, and troubles from a sicke soule, were sicke themselues of feare worthy to be laughed at.

text.DRB: For although none of the monsters disturbed them: being moved with the passing by of beasts, and hissing of serpents, they perished trembling: and denying that they saw the air which by no means any man could avoid.
text.KJV: 17:9 For though no terrible thing did feare them: yet being skared with beasts that passed by, and hissing of serpents,

text.DRB: For whereas wickedness is fearful, it giveth testimony of condemnation: for a troubled conscience doth always presume cruel things.
text.KJV: 17:10 They died for feare, denying that they saw the ayre, which could of no side be auoided.
17:11 For wickednesse condemned by her owne witnesse, is very timorous, and being pressed with conscience, alwayes forecasteth grieuous things.
text.NABRE: 17:11 For wickedness, of its nature cowardly, testifies in its own condemnation, and because of a distressed conscience, always magnifies misfortunes.
text.NRSV: For wickedness is a cowardly thing, condemned by its own testimony; distressed by conscience, it has always exaggerated the difficulties.
text.DRB: For fear is nothing else but a betraying of the aids of cogitation.

text.KJV: 17:12 For feare is nothing else, but a betraying of the succours which reason offereth.
text.NABRE: 17:12 For fear is nought but the surrender of the helps that come from reason;
text.NRSV: For fear is nothing but a surrender of the helps that come from reason.
text.DRB: And whilst inwardly there is less expectation, the greater doth he count the ignorance of that cause which maketh the torment.
But they that during the night indeed impotent, and coming upon them from the lowest and highest hell, slept the same sleep,
text.KJV: 17:13 And the expectation from within being lesse, counteth the ignorance more then the cause which bringeth the torment.

text.DRB: were sometime molested with the fear of monsters, sometime failed by passing away of the soul: for sudden fear and unlooked for came upon them.
text.KJV: 17:14 But they sleeping the same sleepe that night which was indeed intolerable, and which came vpon them out of the bottomes of ineuitable hell:

text.DRB: Moreover if any of them had fallen down, he was kept shut up in prison without iron.
text.KJV: 17:15 Were partly vexed with monstrous apparitions, and partly fainted, their heart failing them: for a suddaine feare and not looked for, came vpon them.

text.DRB: For if one were a husbandman, or if a shepherd, or worker of the labours in the field were suddenly taken, he sustained necessity inevitable.
text.KJV: 17:16 So then, whosoeuer there fell downe, was straitly kept, shut vp in a prison without yron barres.

text.DRB: For with one chain of darkness they were all tied together. Whether it were the hissing wind, or among the thick boughs of trees the sweet sound of birds, or the force of water running downward exceedingly,
text.KJV: 17:17 For whether hee were husbandman, or shepheard, or a labourer in the field, he was ouertaken, and endured that necessitie, which could not be auoided: for they were all bound with one chaine of darkenesse.

text.DRB: or the mighty sound of rocks tumbled headlong, or the running of playing beasts, that were not seen, or the mighty noise of roaring beasts, or an Echo resounding from the highest mountains: they made them swoon for fear.
text.KJV: 17:18 Whether it were a whistling winde, or a melodious noise of birdes among the spreading branches, or a pleasing fall of water running violently:

text.DRB: For all the world was illuminated with a clear light, & none was hindered in their works.
text.KJV: 17:19 Or a terrible sound of stones cast downe, or a running that could not be seene of skipping beasts, or a roaring voice of most sauage wilde beasts, or a rebounding Eccho from the hollow mountaines: these things made them to swoone for feare.

text.DRB: But over them only was laid an heavy night, the image of darkness, which was to come upon them. They therefore were unto themselves more heavy than the darkness.
But to thy saints there was very great light, and their voice indeed they heard, but figure they saw not. And because themselves also did not suffer by the same things, they magnified thee:
text.KJV: 18:1 Neuerthelesse, thy Saints had a very great light, whose voice they hearing and not seeing their shape, because they also had not suffered the same things, they counted them happy.

text.DRB: and they that before had been hurt, because they were not hurt, gave thanks: and that there might be a difference, they asked a gift.
text.KJV: 18:2 But for that they did not hurt them now, of whom they had beene wronged before, they thanked them, and besought them pardon, for that they had beene enemies.

text.DRB: For the which cause they had a burning pillar of fire for a guide of the unknown way, and thou gavest them the sun without hurt of a good harbour.
text.KJV: 18:3 In stead whereof thou gauest them a burning pillar of fire, both to be a guide of the vnknowen iourney, and an harmelesse Sunne to entertaine them honourably.

text.DRB: They indeed worthy to lack light, and to suffer the prison of darkness, which kept thy children shut up, by whom the uncorrupt light of the law began to be given to the world.
text.KJV: 18:4 For they were worthy to be depriued of light, and imprisoned in darknesse, who had kept thy sonnes shut vp, by whom the vncorrupt light of the law was to be giuen vnto the world.

text.DRB: When they decreed to kill the infants of the just: and one child being laid forth, and delivered, thou to the reproving of them, didst take away a multitude of children, and destroyedst them together in the mighty water.
text.KJV: 18:5 And when they had determined to slay the babes of the Saints, one child being cast forth, and saued: to reproue them, thou tookest away the multitude of their children, and destroyedst them altogether in a mightie water.

text.DRB: For that night was known before of our fathers, that they knowing indeed what oaths they had credited, might be of better comfort.
text.KJV: 18:6 Of that night were our fathers certified afore, that assuredly knowing vnto what oathes they had giuen credence, they might afterwards bee of good cheere.

text.DRB: And by thy people indeed the health of the just was received, but destruction by the unjust.
text.KJV: 18:7 So of thy people was accepted both the saluation of the righteous, and destruction of the enemies.

text.DRB: For as thou didst hurt the adversaries: so us also thou didst magnify, provoking us.
text.KJV: 18:8 For wherewith thou didst punish our aduersaries, by the same thou didst glorifie vs whom thou hadst called.

text.DRB: For the just children of the good sacrificed secretly, and disposed the law of justice in concord: that the just should receive both good & evil alike, singing now the praises of the fathers.
text.KJV: 18:9 For the righteous children of good men did sacrifice secretly, and with one consent made a holy lawe, that the Saints should bee alike partakers of the same good and euill, the fathers now singing out the songs of praise.

text.DRB: But there sounded a disagreeing voice of the enemies, and a lamentable mourning was heard for the bewailed infants.
text.KJV: 18:10 But on the other side there sounded an ill-according crie of the enemies, and a lamentable noise was caried abroad for children that were bewailed.

text.DRB: And the servant with the master was afflicted with like punishment, and a man of the vulgar sort suffered the like things to the king.
text.KJV: 18:11 The master and the seruaunt were punished after one maner, and like as the king, so suffered the common person.

text.DRB: All therefore alike by one name of death had dead ones innumerable. For neither did the living suffice to bury them: because in one moment, that which was the nobler nation of them, was destroyed.
text.KJV: 18:12 So they altogether had innumerable dead with one kind of death, neither were the liuing sufficient to burie them: for in one moment the noblest ofspring of them was destroyed.

text.DRB: For concerning all things being incredulous because of the enchantments, but then first when there was destruction of the first-begotten, they promised to be the people of God.
text.KJV: 18:13 For whereas they would not beleeue any thing by reason of the enchantments, vpon the destruction of the first borne, they acknowledged this people to be the sonnes of God.
18:14 For while all things were in quiet silence, and that night was in the midst of her swift course,
text.NABRE: 18:14 For when peaceful stillness encompassed everything and the night in its swift course was half spent,
text.NRSV: For while gentle silence enveloped all things, and night in its swift course was now half gone,
text.DRB: For when quiet silence contained all things, and the night was in the mid-way of her course,

text.KJV: 18:15 Thine almighty word leapt downe from heauen, out of thy royall throne, as a fierce man of warre into the midst of a land of destruction,
text.NABRE: 18:15 Your all-powerful word from heaven’s royal throne leapt into the doomed land,
text.NRSV: your all-powerful word leaped from heaven, from the royal throne, into the midst of the land that was doomed, a stern warrior
text.DRB: thy omnipotent word sallying out of heaven from the royal seats, lighted as a severe conqueror upon the midst of the land of destruction,

text.KJV: 18:16 And brought thine vnfained commandement as a sharpe sword, and standing vp filled all things with death, and it touched the heauen, but it stood vpon the earth.
text.NABRE: 18:16 a fierce warrior bearing the sharp sword of your inexorable decree, And alighted, and filled every place with death, and touched heaven, while standing upon the earth.
text.NRSV: carrying the sharp sword of your authentic command, and stood and filled all things with death, and touched heaven while standing on the earth.
text.DRB: a sharp sword carrying thy unfeigned commandment, and standing filled all with death, and reached even to heaven standing on the earth.
Then incontinent the visions of naughty dreams troubled them, and fears unlooked for came upon them.
text.KJV: 18:17 Then suddenly visions of horrible dreames troubled them sore, and terrours came vpon them vnlooked for.

text.DRB: And one here another there cast forth half alive, shewed for what cause of death he died.
text.KJV: 18:18 And one throwen here, another there halfe dead, shewed the cause of his death.

text.DRB: For the visions that troubled them, forewarned these things, that they might not perish as ignorant why they suffered evils.
text.KJV: 18:19 For the dreames that troubled them, did foreshew this, lest they should perish, and not know why they were afflicted.

text.DRB: But then there touched the just also a tentation of death, and a disturbance of the multitude was made in the wilderness: but thy wrath did not long continue.
text.KJV: 18:20 Yea, the tasting of death touched the righteous also, and there was a destruction of the multitude in the wildernes: but the wrath endured not long.

text.DRB: For a man without blame hastening to pray for the people, bringing forth the shield of his ministry, prayer, and by incense alleging supplication, resisted the wrath, and made an end of the necessity, shewing that he is thy servant.
text.KJV: 18:21 For then the blamelesse man made haste, and stood foorth to defend them, and bringing the shield of his proper ministerie, euen prayer and the propitiation of incense, set himselfe against the wrath, and so brought the calamity to an end, declaring that hee was thy seruant.

text.DRB: And he overcame the multitudes, not in strength of body, nor with might of armour, but with a word subdued he him that vexed him, rehearsing the oaths of the parents, and the testament.
text.KJV: 18:22 So hee ouercame the destroyer, not with strength of body, nor force of armes, but with a word subdued he him that punished, alleaging the oathes and couenants made with the fathers.

text.DRB: For when they were now fallen dead by heaps one upon another, he stood between, and cut off the violence, and separated that way which leadeth to the living.
text.KJV: 18:23 For when the dead were now fallen downe by heaps one vpon another, standing betweene, he staied the wrath, and parted the way to the liuing.

text.DRB: For in the vesture down to the foot, which he had, was all the world: and the glorious things of the fathers were graven in the four jewels of stones, & thy magnificence was written in the diadem of his head.
text.KJV: 18:24 For in the long garment was the whole world, & in the foure rowes of the stones was the glory of the fathers grauen, and thy maiestie vpon the diademe of his head.

text.DRB: And to these he that destroyed gave peace, & these did he fear: for the proof only of wrath was sufficient.
text.KJV: 18:25 Vnto these the destroyer gaue place, and was afraid of them: for it was enough that they onely tasted of the wrath.

text.DRB: But upon the impious even to the later end there came wrath without mercy. For he foreknew also the things that should come unto them:
text.KJV: 19:1 As for the vngodly, wrath came vpon them without mercie vnto the end: for he knew before what they would doe;

text.DRB: because when they had permitted that they should depart, and had sent them away with great diligence, they repenting pursued them.
text.KJV: 19:2 Howe that hauing giuen them leaue to depart, and sent them hastily away, they would repent and pursue them.

text.DRB: For having as yet mourning between their hands, and lamenting at the graves of the dead, they took to themselves another cogitation of folly: and whom by entreating they had cast forth, them they pursued as fugitives:
text.KJV: 19:3 For whilest they were yet mourning, and making lamentation at the graues of the dead, they added another foolish deuice, and pursued them as fugitiues, whom they had entreated to be gone.

text.DRB: for worthy necessity brought them to this end: and they lost the remembrance of those things which had chanced, that punishment might fulfill the things that wanted to the torments:
text.KJV: 19:4 For the destiny, whereof they were worthy, drew them vnto this end, and made them forget the things that had already happened, that they might fulfill the punishment which was wanting to their torments,

text.DRB: and that thy people certes might pass through marvelously, but they might find a new death.
text.KJV: 19:5 And that thy people might passe a wonderfull way: but they might find a strange death.

text.DRB: For every creature according to his kind was fashioned again from the beginning, serving thy precepts, that thy children might be kept without hurt.
text.KJV: 19:6 For the whole creature in his proper kind was fashioned againe anew, seruing the peculiar commandements that were giuen vnto them, that thy children might be kept without hurt.

text.DRB: For a cloud overshadowed their camp, and out of the water which was before, there appeared dry land, and in the red sea a way without impediment, and of the great depth a springing field:
text.KJV: 19:7 As namely, a cloud shadowing the campe, and where water stood before drie land appeared, and out of the red Sea a way without impediment, and out of the violent streame a greene field:

text.DRB: through the which all the nation passed, which was protected with thy hand, seeing thy marvelous things and wonders.
text.KJV: 19:8 Where-thorough all the people went that were defended with thy hand, seeing thy marueilous strange wonders.
19:9 For they went at large like horses, and leaped like lambes, praising thee O Lord, who hadst deliuered them.
text.NABRE: 19:9 For they ranged about like horses, and leapt like lambs, praising you, Lord, their deliverer.
text.NRSV: For they ranged like horses, and leaped like lambs, praising you, O Lord, who delivered them.
text.DRB: For even as horses they fed on meat, and as lambs they rejoiced, magnifying thee o Lord, which didst deliver them.
For they were mindful of those things, which had been done in their sojourning, how for the nation of beasts the earth brought forth flies, & for fishes the river yielded a multitude of frogs.
text.KJV: 19:10 For they were yet mindefull of the things that were done while they soiourned in the strange land, how the ground brought forth flies in stead of cattell, and how the riuer cast vp a multitude of frogs in stead of fishes.

text.DRB: And last of all they saw a new creature of birds, when allured by concupiscence they desired meats of deliciousness.
text.KJV: 19:11 But afterwards they saw a new generation of foules, when being led with their appetite they asked delicate meates.

text.DRB: For in comfort of their desire, there came up to them the quail from the sea: and vexations came upon the sinners, not without those arguments, which were made before by the force of lightnings: for they suffered justly according to their wickedness.
text.KJV: 19:12 For quailes came vp vnto them from the Sea, for their contentment.

text.DRB: For they instituted a more detestable inhospitality: some certes received not the unknown strangers, and other-some brought the good strangers into servitude.
text.KJV: 19:13 And punishments came vpon the sinners not without former signes by the force of thunders: for they suffered iustly, according to their owne wickednesse, insomuch as they vsed a more hard and hatefull behauiour towards strangers:

text.DRB: And not only these things, but indeed there was another respect also of them: for they against their will received the strangers.
text.KJV: 19:14 For the Sodomits did not receiue those whom they knew not when they came: but these brought friends into bondage, that had well deserued of them.

text.DRB: But they that received them with gladness, did afflict them with most cruel sorrows, that used the same rights:
text.KJV: 19:15 And not onely so: but peraduenture some respect shall be had of those, because they vsed strangers not friendly.

text.DRB: but they were strucken with blindness: as they in the doors of the just, when they were covered with sudden darkness, every man sought the passage of his door.
text.KJV: 19:16 But these very grieuously afflicted them, whom they had receiued with feastings, and were already made partakers of the same lawes with them.

text.DRB: For whilst the elements are turned in themselves, as in an instrument the sound of the quality is changed, and all keep their sound: wherefore it may be certainly judged by the very sight.
text.KJV: 19:17 Therefore euen with blindnesse were these stricken, as those were at the doores of the righteous man: when being compassed about with horrible great darknesse, euery one sought the passage of his owne doores.

19:18 For the elements were changed in themselues by a kind of harmonie, like as in a Psaltery notes change the name of the tune, and yet are alwayes sounds, which may well be perceiued by the sight of the things that haue beene done.
text.NABRE: 19:18 For the elements, in ever-changing harmony, like strings of the harp, produce new melody, while the flow of music steadily persists. And this can be perceived exactly from a review of what took place.
text.NRSV: For the elements changed places with one another, as on a harp the notes vary the nature of the rhythm, while each note remains the same.
text.DRB: For the things of the field were turned into things of the water: and what soever were swimming things passed into the land.
The fire had force in water above his power, and the water forgot her quenching nature.
text.KJV: 19:19 For earthly things were turned into watry, and the things that before swamme in the water, now went vpon the ground.

text.DRB: On the contrary the flames vexed not the flesh of corruptible beasts walking therewith, neither did they melt that good meat, which was easily dissolved even as ice. For in all things thou didst magnify thy people o Lord, and didst honour them, and didst not despise them, at all time, and in every place assisting them.

text.KJV: 19:22 For in all things, O Lord, thou didst magnifie thy people, and glorifie them, neither didst thou lightly regard them: but didst assist them in euery time and place.
text.NABRE: 19:22 For every way, Lord! you magnified and glorified your people; unfailing, you stood by them in every time and circumstance.
text.NRSV: For in everything, O Lord, you have exalted and glorified your people, and you have not neglected to help them at all times and in all places.
